era_context: The Seven Years' War (1756–1763) is in its third year, with the conflict expanding and intensifying across multiple continents. In Europe, Prussia under Frederick II faces pressure from Russia, Austria, and France, but manages to hold its own through superior military tactics. In North America, the British are beginning to gain the upper hand against the French. The Enlightenment is in full swing, with major intellectual figures active across Europe.

events.title: Battle of Zorndorf
events.description: Prussian forces under Frederick II (Frederick the Great) engage Russian forces under General Peter Saltykov in a major battle near the Oder River. The battle is fiercely contested and results in heavy casualties on both sides. While the Prussians claim a tactical victory, the Russians are not decisively defeated and continue to pose a threat to Prussia. The battle demonstrates the intensity of the Seven Years' War in Eastern Europe and the vulnerability of Prussia despite Frederick's military genius.
key_figures: Frederick II (Frederick the Great, Prussian king)

sources.name: Prussian military records and Frederick's own writings

events.certainty_note: The battle date (August 25, 1758) and outcome are well-documented in multiple contemporary sources.

events.title: Battle of Carillon (Ticonderoga)
events.region: Lake Champlain, New York, North America
events.description: French forces under General Louis-Joseph de Montcalm defeat a much larger British force under General James Abercromby at Fort Carillon (later renamed Ticonderoga by the British). The French, despite being outnumbered, use defensive fortifications and disciplined musket fire to repel repeated British assaults. The battle is a rare French victory in North America during the Seven Years' War and demonstrates the effectiveness of French defensive tactics. However, the French cannot hold the fort indefinitely, and it will be captured by the British in 1759.
events.certainty_note: The battle date (July 8, 1758) and outcome are precisely documented in multiple contemporary sources.

events.title: British capture of Louisbourg
events.region: Cape Breton Island, Nova Scotia, North America
events.description: British forces under General Jeffrey Amherst and Admiral Edward Boscawen capture the French fortress of Louisbourg after a siege lasting from June to July 1758. Louisbourg is a major French stronghold and an important base for French naval operations in North America. Its capture is a significant British victory and opens the way for British operations against Quebec and other French territories. The fortress is subsequently destroyed to prevent its recapture.
events.certainty_note: The siege dates (June–July 1758) and outcome are well-documented in British and French military records.
cross_references: Leads to British operations against Quebec (1759)

events.title: Battle of Plassey aftermath and consolidation of British power in Bengal
events.description: Following the British victory at Plassey in 1757, British forces under Robert Clive consolidate their control over Bengal in 1758. The British establish administrative control over the region and begin to extract revenue and resources. This consolidation marks the beginning of British territorial dominance in India and sets the stage for the expansion of British power across the subcontinent over the next century.
key_figures: Mir Jafar (Nawab of Bengal, British ally)
events.certainty_note: The consolidation of British power in Bengal in 1758 is well-documented in East India Company records.
cross_references: Leads to expansion of British power in India

events.title: Publication of Diderot and d'Alembert's Encyclopédie (continued publication)
events.description: Denis Diderot and Jean le Rond d'Alembert continue publication of the Encyclopédie, a comprehensive work of Enlightenment thought and knowledge. The Encyclopédie is one of the most important intellectual projects of the 18th century and represents the culmination of Enlightenment ideals of reason, progress, and the democratization of knowledge. The work is controversial due to its critique of religious authority and traditional institutions, and it faces censorship and suppression by the French government and the Catholic Church.
events.certainty_note: The publication of the Encyclopédie is well-documented. Volume 7 was published in 1758.
cross_references: Part of broader Enlightenment intellectual movement
Influences European thought and leads to French Revolution

events.title: Continued Seven Years' War: Mixed results across theaters
events.description: 1758 is a year of mixed military results in the Seven Years' War. While Britain achieves significant victories in North America (Louisbourg) and India, Prussia faces continued pressure from Russia and Austria in Europe. France achieves a notable victory at Carillon but is losing ground overall. The war continues to expand and intensify, with no clear end in sight. Naval operations continue to favor Britain, which is establishing dominance over French sea lanes.
key_figures: William Pitt the Elder (British Prime Minister)
events.certainty_note: The overall pattern of military operations in 1758 is well-documented.

events.title: Death of Jonathan Edwards
events.description: Jonathan Edwards, the influential American theologian and philosopher, dies on March 22, 1758, at age 54. Edwards is one of the most important intellectual figures in colonial America and a key figure in the Great Awakening religious revival. His theological writings and philosophical works have shaped American Protestant thought and continue to influence American intellectual life. His death marks the end of an era in American colonial intellectual history.
events.certainty_note: Edwards's death date (March 22, 1758) is precisely documented.
cross_references: Part of broader American colonial intellectual history
Influences subsequent American theological and philosophical thought

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Carillon is sometimes presented as a clear French victory, but modern scholarship emphasizes that while the French won the tactical engagement, they were unable to hold the fort indefinitely due to supply shortages and the broader strategic situation. The British eventually captured Ticonderoga in 1759. The narrative of French military competence at Carillon should be balanced against the overall pattern of French military decline during the Seven Years' War. Additionally, the consolidation of British power in Bengal in 1758 is sometimes portrayed as a smooth process, but it involved significant resistance from local populations and administrative challenges.
historiographic_note: 1758 is well-documented in British, French, Prussian, and Russian sources due to the Seven Years' War. Military operations are extensively documented in official records, dispatches, and contemporary accounts. Cultural events (publication of the Encyclopédie, death of Jonathan Edwards) are well-recorded in European and American sources. The historical record is heavily weighted toward military and political events; economic and social history is less extensively documented. Colonial and indigenous perspectives are underrepresented in surviving sources. The East India Company records provide important documentation of British activities in India, though they reflect British perspectives.

graph_edges.note: Battle of Zorndorf precedes Battle of Kunersdorf (1759)
French victory at Carillon is followed by British capture of Ticonderoga (1759)
Capture of Louisbourg opens way for British operations against Quebec
British consolidation in Bengal contributes to British dominance in India
Encyclopédie influences Enlightenment thought leading to French Revolution
